skill.name: workflow-automation
skill.description: Helps design reliable workflow automation using durable execution, orchestration patterns, and platform tradeoff analysis.
skill.summary: Plan production-grade workflow automation across n8n, Temporal, Inngest, queues, and scheduled jobs.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported four Ruby or shell backtick execution findings on SKILL.md line 68 and one weak cryptographic algorithm finding on line 3. Manual review found only Markdown inline code and descriptive text, with no executable shell code, crypto API, prompt injection, network access, or data exfiltration evidence.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 68 lists related skill names inside Markdown inline backticks. There is no Ruby code, shell invocation, command construction, or execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line contains only inline Markdown labels for related skills. No executable language context or command runner is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Finding Has No Code Evidence
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 3 is frontmatter description text about reliable workflow automation. It does not mention or implement MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any other cryptographic algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported location is descriptive metadata, not code. I found no cryptographic API usage in the reviewed file.

content.value_statement: Workflow automation can fail when long-running tasks lose state or retry unsafe work. This skill helps teams choose patterns and platforms for durable, observable automation.
actual_capabilities: Compares n8n, Temporal, and Inngest tradeoffs for workflow automation decisions.
Explains sequential, parallel, and orchestrator-worker workflow patterns.
Highlights durable execution needs for payments and external service calls.
Identifies production concerns such as idempotency, timeouts, retries, and observability.
Covers background jobs, scheduled tasks, queues, and event-driven workflows.
limitations: Does not provide runnable platform templates or deployment scripts.
Does not replace vendor documentation for exact API behavior.
Does not audit existing workflow code automatically.
Does not choose a platform without project requirements and operational constraints.

use_cases.title: Select an Automation Platform
use_cases.description: Compare n8n, Temporal, and Inngest for reliability, complexity, and team fit.

use_cases.title: Improve Background Job Reliability
use_cases.description: Plan retries, idempotency, checkpoints, and observability for long-running jobs.

use_cases.title: Design Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Structure AI agent tasks into sequential, parallel, or orchestrator-worker workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Platform
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between n8n, Temporal, and Inngest for a workflow that runs [describe workflow]. Compare reliability, complexity, and operations.

prompt_templates.title: Map Workflow Steps
prompt_templates.prompt: Break this workflow into durable steps: [describe workflow]. Identify sequential steps, parallel steps, retries, timeouts, and required checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Review Reliability Risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this workflow plan for reliability risks: [paste plan]. Focus on idempotency, side effects, observability, retries, and failure recovery.

prompt_templates.title: Design Orchestrator-Worker Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an orchestrator-worker workflow for [system]. Define worker responsibilities, event boundaries, retry policies, failure handling, and monitoring signals.

output_examples.input: Choose a platform for customer onboarding with email, billing, CRM, and approval steps.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: Inngest for a developer-led team that needs durable steps without Temporal-level operational complexity. Use idempotency keys for billing and checkpoint each external call.

output_examples.input: Review a payment workflow that charges a card, creates an invoice, and sends a receipt.
output_examples.output: Key risks: payment calls need idempotency keys, each side effect needs a checkpoint, and receipt sending should retry independently after invoice creation succeeds.

output_examples.input: Plan a workflow that enriches leads from three independent data providers.
output_examples.output: Use a parallel pattern for provider lookups, aggregate successful results, set per-provider timeouts, and record partial failures for follow-up.

best_practices: Use idempotency keys for every external side effect.
Break long workflows into checkpointed steps with clear retry boundaries.
Add observability for step status, retries, failures, and recovery paths.
anti_patterns: Do not run payment workflows without durable execution.
Do not place unrelated business processes into one monolithic workflow.
Do not store large payloads directly inside workflow state.

faq.question: What platforms does this skill discuss?
faq.answer: It discusses n8n, Temporal, and Inngest, with attention to reliability and operational tradeoffs.

faq.question: Can it generate production workflow code?
faq.answer: It provides architecture guidance and prompts, but it does not include runnable code templates.

faq.question: Is this useful for AI agent workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on durable execution patterns that help AI agent tasks recover from failures.

faq.question: Does it require a specific cloud provider?
faq.answer: No. The guidance is platform-oriented and does not depend on one cloud vendor.

faq.question: What reliability topics does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers idempotency, checkpoints, retries, timeouts, observability, and failure recovery.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Backend engineers, automation builders, and technical leads planning reliable workflow systems can use it.
